CURRENT AFFAIRS.

We received by post a printed communication
headed ** Tand Sebtlement, for Soldiers.”” The words,
1o the Editor of '’ are printed, and the words, the
IEADER, are filled in by hand in ink. We cast it aside
as one of those many circulars which come to news-
papers : some temporary clerk, we suppose, is given a
pen and ink and a Press directory and told to send the
circulars to all papers. It was by chance that subse-
quently we glanced at it again, and our eye—did not
someone accuse us ab some time or other of having an
‘“ eagle eye,’”’ whatever that is—was attracted by the
name * Horace Plunkett.” What’s this latest game
of this Generalissimo of the Hairy Tairies? This new

ame is, needless to say, partly an appeal for funds:
fhe other object is to find jobs for discarded or retired
Tommies. Retired Tommies and the need of *“ trained
agriculturists *’ suggest the * Land Settlement for

Soldiers " to. Sir Horace of the Tairies, and others,
and is another excuse for Sir Horace to appeal for
funds. How will the Irish farmers, nay, even that
fuiry host, the ‘* 100,000 organised farmers of Ireland,”
like the following :—

It is unquestionable that, if cultivated on
scientific and business lines, the land can be made
to pay. Moreover, it offers the soldier a career
in wh he will not be handicapped by serious
competition and for which his Army training,
physical and mental, peculiarly fits him.

Teaching the Tommies to farm may afford new open-
ings for the Hairy Fairy, for Adams and for Anderson,
if the I.A.0.8. goes bang; indeed, it might coax
Brother Richmond Noble back to the fold of agricul-
tural regeneration again.

The country is bad enough as it is, but what would
it be if we had the vile-spoken chaps that disgrace
O’Connell Street peppered over the country as farmers
and farm workers? The circular says:—

The actual work of the Association will consist
in qualifying soldiers for settlement or employ-
ment on the land either at home, or, when their
liability to recall as Reservists has terminated, in
the oversea Empire, and afterwards in finding
openings for them. It is intended, as the first
step, to establish training farms at home where
the men, preferably during the period of service in
the Reserve, will acquire knowledge of agricul-
ture and crafts which will be useful to them as
colonists, farmers or employés. The practical
application to agi ulture of co-operative methods,

, in these days essential to the success
of working farmers, will be taught. The Associa-
tion will be prepared to work with the Agricultural
Organization Societics and with all voluntary
agencies concerned either with emigration to
British possessions abroad or with rural develop-
ment at home, a matter upon which the Board
of Agriculture and Fisl reries may be relied on to
advise, The War Office has been fully informed
of the scheme.

The italics are ours.
field of work for
Tommies to farm.

So this may be taken as a new
the I.A.0.S.—teaching scrapped

This scheme for benefiting Tommies has for one of
its Vice-Presidents Forace Plunkett. There are
several real live lords in the swim too, and they ask
us to allow.them to appeal for funds in our columns.
They may appeal, but we advise our readers to send
their money to the fund for the relief of the victims
of savage anti-Catholic and anti-Home Rule bigotry
in Bolfast. We don’t think that this teaching Tommy
to farm enterprise with which Horace Plunkett is
conneeted will catch-on in this country.

upon him.

(Enter a procession of Sawbones bearing black flags, -
on whieh are painted skulls and cross-bones, and led

Doctors old and fresh,
Curers, killers, stabbers,
Have your pound of flesh,
Act like proper grabbers.
Not a bone we'll set; :
Cramps may hold their station
If we cannot get
Sawbones’ valuation,

Chorus.
No surrender there,
No negotiation.
We munst have our share—
Sawbones’ valuation.

Mick.—THo, you up there. :
Hear that and tremble like a palsied man.
I'd like to feel your pulse.

Pres.—No, thank you, Sawbones.
I do assure you that our pulses beat
With normal vigour, and we do require
No visit from a doctor.

Mick.—Uncultured creatures of the common brood,
Who never travel in a motor car,
Or never feel the Anglicising joy
That comes from contact with the classy *“ Saved,””
We want to know if you’ll consent to pay
The biggest fee to Sawbones for his work
In chasing microbes from your systems off,
And patching up your constitutions well
With pills and sticking plaster.

N SSEII

Pres.—Throw physic to the dogs
While Sawbones on his body-mending skill
Puts Shylock values.

Mick.—Oh man, I fear thy brain doth suffer much
I'rom some derangement of the mental parts.
I’ll make a diagnosis of the same
If thou wilt pay. me——

PrES.—Go cure thine own swelled head.

Mick.—I give thee up; thou art incurable,
Tall in the Sawbones, and prepare to march
Unto the next place on the doctors’ list
Tor fee enforcement.
Exeunt Sawbones.

The Sunday Orchestral Concerts, which have become
& necessity of musical life in the city, will resume on
next Sunday’in the Antient Concert Rooms, at 4 p.m.,
under the conductorship of Signor Hsposito. ~ The
programme for the occasion is very interesting, com-
prising works by Rossini, Mozart, Albanesi, Rubenstein
and Beethoven.  An imporlant change has been
made in the orchestra owing {o the departure
from Dublin of Herr Adolf Wilhelmj. Signor A.
Simonetti, the eminent violinist and composer, who
has recently been appointed Professor of the Violin at
the R.ILA.M., will in future lead the ovchestra, and
will, on next Sunday, contribute several violin solos.
Miss Nettie Edwards, the well-known soprano will sing
several numbers.

wooden rifle affair has been a sad failure eferring

resist Home Rule by force caused Asquith to remark:—

I need not tell you, gentlemen—I need not tell

We see by an advertisement in a Unionist daily paper.
that ‘‘ The Petition against the Home Rule Bill ** can
be signed at the office of the City of Dublin Unionist
Association any day this week. The Petition! And
has the Covenant now become a Petition. But more
spicy still is a phrase which Saint Carson ‘used in &
letter he wrote to ‘“ My Dear Bates.” The phrase is:
““ Our precious Covenant.”’  Well, that about hits it
off; we feel sure that many of them must by this be
perceiving the ludicrous side of that precious Cove-
nant of theirs.

In the cowrse of a letter, chiefly concerning another
matter, a correspondent incidentally remarks:—*“ Keep
at the doctors, particularly the mean Catholics, who
never earn a sixpence from their Masonic friends, who
now are compelled to crush the masses with more taxa-
tion, as this is really what it means for those who are
members of the Friendly Societies, and this, as usual,
hits our people more than the other side.  The mis-
fortune is, you did not criticise them sooner, because
if you had some of them would have a little back-
bone to stand against the present antics of their brothers
in England.”’

The Dublin Friendly Societies Union held a meeting
on Monday night. Mr. J. D. Nugent, General Secre-
tary of the A.0.H., was elected chairman; Mr. A.
Chilton, of the I.A.O.R., was elected sccretary, and on
the committee are members of the Grocers’ Assistants,
I.G. Bottlemakers, Friendly Brothers, Irish National
Foresters, Irish Clerks, Emerald Friendly, D. and S.E.
Railway, Mount Carmel, Irish Nurses. It was decided
that no society should negotiate with the doctors ex-
cept through the Dublin Friendly Societies Union. The
Dublin Corporation is to be asked to cease payments of
grants to the various hospitals unless they agree to
carry out the same conditions as heretofore with regard
to the admission of patients. At this meeting repre-
sentatives attended from a large number of societies,
and it would seem that the Dublin Sawbones are up
against a great deal more than they probably bargained
for. It is intended to hold a public meeting in the

T O open whitened sepulchres must always be most

unsavoury and disagreeable to the persons
employed at such work and to the bystanders. How-
ev ometimes such disagreeable work becomes a
n sity. We Catholics of the North have always kept
silence about the gross intolerance of which we

were the victims. We imagined that in so
doing we were making the way ecasier for the
peacemaker. We were outraged in our most sacred

convictions; we were hurt by the insults offered to our
clergy, we were shocked at the sacrilegious attacks on
our churches, we were scandalized by the references
to our consecrated virgins, we were indignant at our
children being beaten and tortured coming from and
going to school, and we were so accustomed to vile and
obscene language regarding the venerable head of the
Church that.we considered *‘ Hell rip and roast the

ends stolen out of holy writ, and seem a saint, when

Let us descend to particulars and we will give chap

Street, was working on the Monday morning after Celtic
Park riots, at the shipyard, on a sub-contract for M.
Henry. At the breakfast hour he was approached by
a hundred hooligans, firing nuts and bolts at him.
Andrew considered it was merely a ‘‘ lark,” but on the
heroes marched and demanded that Andrew should go
down on his kuees and curse the Pope.  Andrew’s
reply was: ““ Not if they should kill him."”” Then they
started upon him; they knocked him down and kicked
him; he got up and ran, with the demons after him;
one hit him with a “* clinker ”” and wounded his head,
cufbing it about two inches; a harbour constable came
upon the scene.  Andrew got the blood washed off in
a sort of way, and had to be taken to the Mater Hos-
pital in the ambulance.

PICTURE SHOWS AND SUNDAY DESECRATION.

M HERE is nothing so good that it may not become

debased; nothing so noble that it may not be
perverted to evil ends. To this rule the cinematograph
is no exception. Under the judicious guidance of
capable and patriotic Irishmen, the picture palace
might become a most valuable factor in our national
life. It might create and foster an interest in Irish
history, an appreciation of the loveliness of I
scenery, an enthusiasm for what is best and healthiest
in the activities of the nation.

Views of ancient Irish abbeys—Jerpoint and Melli-
font, Dunbrody and Corcomroc, Sligo Abbey and many
another—might be exhibited upon the screen, and a
few words of explanation devoted to each in turn. In
like manner, the picturesque castles with which the
country is studded—Kilkea and Adare, Blarney, Bun-
rathy, Lemanegh and the rest—might advantageously
be shown and described. Again, the charming ** beauty
spots *’ of Irish scenery might be reproduced in our
“picture shows, instead of being ignominiously
neglected, as they are at present; and thus the youth
of our cities would begin to feel a legitimate pride in
the glorious scenery of their native land. The scenery
of Killarney and Wicklow, Connemara and Donegal—
not to mention a host of other delightful localities—
would furnish some splendid pictures of this kind.

Once more, there are numberless views of Irish
health resorts; of Gaelic colleges; of hurling and foot-
and pastimes; of our
industrial centres and their work; not to speak of

picture shows are to open henceforward on Sundays.
as well as week days. It is not enough that they
should present scenes of blood and crime, revenge and:
illicit love to the impressionable minds of young people

on Sundays also. It is not enough that the minds of
our young people should be defiled on week-days; they

then be in full swing. We shall then have the foes-
of our Faith and nation pointing the finger of scorn at
us for our scandalous inobservance of the Lord’s Day.

checkmated in time, will inflict deadly injury upon:
the morals of our young people.  Everyone knows
